content.problemStatement:
Chef has placed nnn stones on a 2D plane at distinct integer coordinates. Each stone occupies a unique position defined by coordinates (x,y)(x, y)(x,y). 

Chef wants to remove as many stones as possible under a strict rule: a stone can only be removed if there exists another stone in the same row or the same column that has not yet been removed. 

Help Chef determine the maximum number of stones that can be removed while ensuring that at least one stone remains in each connected group of stones sharing rows or columns.

Function DeclarationFunction NamemaxStonesRemovedmaxStonesRemovedmaxStonesRemoved - This function calculates the maximum number of stones that Chef can remove based on the connectivity of stones in rows and columns.

Parameters
stonesstonesstones : A 2D vector where each inner vector contains two integers representing the coordinates [x,y][x, y][x,y] of a stone on the 2D plane.
Each coordinate is a non-negative integer representing the stone's position.
Return Value
Returns a number.
The return value represents the maximum number of stones that can be removed while ensuring that at least one stone remains in each connected group.
Constraints
1≤T≤501 \leq T \leq 501≤T≤50
1≤n≤10001 \leq n \leq 10001≤n≤1000 - number of stones.
0≤xi,yi≤1040 \leq x_i, y_i \leq 10^40≤xi​,yi​≤104 - coordinates of stones.
No two stones share the same coordinate point.

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains an integer nnn — the number of stones.
The next nnn lines each contain two integers xix_ixi​ and yiy_iyi​ — the coordinates of the ithi^{th}ith stone.




Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer — the maximum number of stones Chef can remove while following the rules.
constraints: No two stones share the same coordinate point
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer n, the number of stones. The next n lines each contain two integers x and y, the coordinates of the ith stone.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer — the maximum number of stones Chef can remove while following the rules.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For test case 1: All stones form a connected group through rows and columns, so we can remove 5 stones, leaving 1.
For test case 2: Stones form three connected groups; we remove stones in each group except one, totaling 3.
For test case 3: Only one stone exists, so no stones can be removed, output is 0.

Explanation:
For t=1: The stones form a 2x2 grid with all connected through rows or columns. We can remove 3 stones, leaving one isolated stone behind.
For t=2: All stones connect through overlapping rows and columns forming one group. We can remove 4 stones, leaving just one stone isolated.

Explanation:
Test case 1: Each stone is isolated in its own row and column, so no stones can be removed. Output is 0.
Test case 2: All stones form a connected group through shared rows and columns, allowing removal of 6 stones while leaving one. Output is 6.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by treating each stone as a node in a graph and using a Union-Find data structure to find the connected components. We iterate over each stone and union its row and column. Then, we count the number of connected components and subtract this from the total number of stones to get the maximum number of stones that can be removed.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n * alpha(n)) per test case, where alpha(n) is the inverse Ackermann function, which grows very slowly. The total time complexity is O(T * n * alpha(n)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n + 10^4) — we need to store the parent array for the Union-Find data structure.
